author: George Orwell
prompt:
Question: Which of the following passages is verbatim from the "1984" book by George Orwell?
Options:
A. There were instances when the abuse continued ceaselessly, and the intolerable thing for him was not that the guards persisted in beating him but that he could not force himself to black out. There were moments when he was so frightened that he began begging for mercy before the beating even started, just seeing a fist raised to hit him made him confess to real and made-up crimes. There were other times when he began determined to confess nothing, when every word had to be wrung out of him between pained gasps for breath, and there were times when he weakly tried to compromise, telling himself: 'I'll confess, but later.'
B. There were moments when the torment went on and on until the heartless, evil, unpardonable act seemed to him not that the guards kept hitting him but that he couldn't make himself lose consciousness. There were times when he was so terrified that he started pleading for mercy before the beating even began, just seeing a fist poised to strike was enough to make him confess to real and fabricated crimes. There were other occasions when he started out resolved to confess nothing, when every word had to be wrenched out of him between gasps of agony, and there were times when he feebly tried to strike a deal, thinking: 'I'll confess, but not right now.'  
C. There were occasions when the torture continued endlessly, and the unbearable thing for him was not that the guards kept beating him but that he could not make himself pass out. There were times when he was so afraid that he began screaming for mercy before the beating even started, just seeing a fist ready to strike him made him confess to crimes real and imagined. There were other times when he began with the determination to confess nothing, when every word had to be forced out of him between pained gasps, and there were times when he weakly tried to compromise, telling himself: 'I will confess, but not yet.'
D. There were times when it went on and on until the cruel, wicked, unforgivable thing seemed to him not that the guards continued to beat him but that he could not force hirnself into losing consciousness. There were times when his nerve so forsook him that he began shouting for mercy even before the beating began, when the mere sight of a fist drawn back for a blow was enough to make him pour forth a confession of real and imaginary crimes. There were other times when he started out with the resolve of confessing nothing, when every word had to be forced out of him between gasps of pain, and there were times when he feebly tried to compromise, when he said to himself: 'I will confess, but not yet.
Answer:
